author: @johnbraunschweig
text:
A bigger turbine has probably a better generation at slow wind speeds. With a wall of smaller turbines on the other hand, the turbines don't need to be stacked square. You could fit more hexagonal cells in the same space, than square one's.
Unfortunatly, there is not enough wind here, to be of any use.

author: @stevemcdowell8829
text:
Nice one Robert. An Archimedes HWAT with generation at the perimeter prompts 3 questions;
1. How does the output compare with a generation via a central shaft? (Not easy to devise a setup that will give a like for like comparison because in one case the generator is integral with the wind catcher and in the other they are separate).
2. What is the best application for the device shown in this video? The Archimedes works most effectively when turned into the wind. If placed in a unidirectional wall, it is much less efficient. Also a wall of Archimedes bricks would have many magnets and coils. Some of your other windwalls, particularly the Ugrinsky version, are less materials intensive and more efficient in a variety of wind conditions. The Archimedes with perimeter generation is probably better suited as a stand alone omnidirectional device.
3. How best to make it omnidirectional? Either use it as HAWT and mount it on a swivel with a vane so that it swings into the wind. Or make it a VAWT mounted on an arrangement of thrust bearings, housed in a Darwen.
The wind wall could look really cool, but the Darwen is the more promising idea as a generator. It also opens up other possibilities which I am working on. Although, by the time that I have drawn them, you will have probably built another 4 prototypes and moved on to something else.! Where do you get your energy and drive from?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: They are all very good questions mate and asking good questions is one point of the video - but another point is to try and have you answer them too - how would you go about that? I only suggest a wind wall because i think they look cool and fit an urban environment - for no other reasons I can see answers to your questions but what I am much more interested in is can you?

replies.author: @stevemcdowell8829
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Robert, thanks for finding time to answer. You are right to challenge me to answer my own questions. Fair play mate. I am working on an idea based on two concentric, contra-rotating cylinders within an omnidirectional windcatcher. You will probably think that I am  "over-complicating matters" again, but I hope to share the concept sketches with you soon.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't think you are over complicating it mate I think sounds like an interesting design i am looking forward to seeing the sketches and thank you for taking my comment in the spirit it was meant - all the best

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: I still want to see better work on a turbine that is omnidirecitonal for wind, but has baffles that redirect the wind hitting the side of it typically that retards the rotation (reduced by having an angled or curved backside to reduce this to get directional rotation) so that instead of it pushing on both sides it keeps the it from undoing what the wind hitting the other side is accomplishing. even if this is only broken up into 3 segments it should allow for an increase in output just from reducing drag in the opposite direction but if it bevels so it hits the correct side, too, even more improvement, and just have the output side moved from the opposite side but rather angle them downward so it pushes all the air out bottom to reduce its weight from dragging on the bearings there, and maybe even place a small enclosure with a fan system down there to allow it to assist power output (using the wind motion twice to generate power LOL)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: if you don't see what you want to see why not give it a go making what you want to see?

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering its in the list of things, but if someone wants to get that internet clout or what ever, I have more than enough to do already I wont be upset that someone took the idea and used it. :) (been working on a way to flux and even extract or scrub an area using energy entirely (no physical items like moving pumps or chemicals) for 3D printing things that cant have oxygen or even have oxygen that needs to be removed like say iron oxide (already got deposition of iron metal on a plastic and a stone surface from pure red iron oxide using this tech but the TiO2 only broke down enough to form larger crystals from the powder, so that is what this next step is really for, once its done though, my next step is to use oxidized and powdered neodymium magnet material to print out custom magnets not only for motors but also generators, and its all using tech that can be gotten off ebay for 20 to 50 USD (collectively each part being just a portion of that :) )

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: interesting thing is my CNC mill uses a similar system to allow my carriage the bed was on to roll on, and had small threaded holes for grub screws so I could adjust each of them to get rid of slack while not tightening so much to lock up the bearings, (did the same thing on larger shafts than I had a bearing to fit into, was to slide it in a sleeve the bearings press inward on and then making them adjustable you can get a large mill fixture with zero or as close as possible slack on it so it does not wobble and such, which means I am betting a larger wind generator could be supported and stablized using smaller bearings that give better result than one large bearing would :)

text:
Hi Rob. I think a modular brick design wired in series has far more potential than simply scaling it up. Also the Archimedes screw turbine is hands down the best way to funnel wind/fluid imo.
ps. Have you found any annoying primitive design limitations using tinkercad?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i do like the wind wall concept especially for urban environments - and yes i have Tinkercad can only handle 200 primitives in any one drawing - complex shapes often require more than that - what i do is draw them up export them and reimport as a single stl - then Tinker cad understands it as one primitive - it's a pain if you have to redesign the complex part

author: @Squire_Chris
text:
Seems like all your content these days 'spins' around 3D printing.
I do a LOT of tinkering... but living off-grid, on the smell of an oily rag, in a shipping container... I ain't got time or money or space for that $**t.
I hope you get back to genuine analogue experimenting again, or I'm out.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well  we all do what we all do - sorry to see you go - all the best

replies.author: @timmyssweet
replies.text:
Time, I understand that.  Money, I’m torn on that.  3d printers have come down a lot, but the cost of everything has gone up.  The basics now seem unaffordable.  We have, must, do more with less, but that’s exactly what Robert is working very hard on.  I really like that this wind generator is under $100 dollars, AUD.

I did some research on using recycled bottles for filament, but I would need 55 for 1 kg of filament.  I get 5c per bottle to recycle.  That around $30.  … the same for 1kg of filament, AUD.  

New tech is nearly cost effective to recycle prototype, but it’s an investment.

Space, well, it is what it is.  I have to fight family members to use the shed, so I gave up.  I now love to design.  I can afford it, and it fulfills my busy mind rather than tinkering, but it’s not as health for my body.  Engine developers don’t build prototypes anymore.  If it works in CAD, they know it will work in real life.  That was said by MAHLE

author: @geoffsimns8424
text: A query from a lessor mind ; Since the cylinder is designed as an aerofoil (English spelling, as opposed to "airfoil" in American) in a cylindrical shape into which an Archimedes screw type system has been mounted, would the cylinder need as much, or a similar amount, of air flow over the outer surface compared to the inner surface for the aerofoil effect to work, otherwise it is just a Archimedes screw type system, given the cylinder is enclosed in a housing that minimises (but does eliminate) airflow over the outer surface? - sorry, that was a bit longwinded (pardon the pun)...

replies.author: @EGDD-42
replies.text: You'll possibly have to change the foil shape along it's length, due to the difference in air volume density. Otherwise you might end up with a "compressor stall" situation

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a good question and one point of the video is to have good questions - but another point is to try and have you answer them too - how would you go about answering that?  I can see answers to your questions but what I am much more interested in is can you and can you investigate it and share your results?

replies.author: @jackheath1416
replies.text:
I always thought not having a printer was limiting me. 

But in reality after some research I saw that they are very inexpensive!  I bought a tiny Tina printer that was recommended on this channel.  It's very easy to learn to use cad and cheaper to make things from pla than most other materials! Plus once you start printing gifts or items you would normally buy they can save you a lot of money as well!

author: @themeek351
text: You could in theory add a magnetic bearing support system with this setup for less resistance!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes you could - the problem with magnetic bearing is they tend to bounce unless you add feedback control

replies.author: @themeek351
replies.text: Yes, but you could mirror the bottom set on top for stability!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @themeek351 certainly - give it a go

author: @marvinschaefer3973
text: I love your videos because they get my brain in a creative state! Did you try without the top bearings to see if it still worked? I wonder if gravity and gyroscopic stability would be enough to keep it from touching the housing and then you have an even simpler version that works better. Also, it seems like you could use the point of a ball point pen on the back as a thrust bearing to save some drag.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I didn't try it mate - but you could and i would love to see what you come up with
